content.text: When a language model needs to return multiple distinct sections in a single response — a reasoning trace, a final answer, a confidence score, and a set of citations — JSON is not always the right container.
uses named paired tags, such as

, to wrap and label regions within the raw text. The result is a structured response that an application can parse field by field, without wrapping every value in quoted JSON strings or escaping embedded newlines.
XML tag-based output is the practice of instructing a language model to delimit distinct sections of its response with opening and closing tags that follow XML syntax:

. Unlike a true XML document, the full response need not be well-formed XML — it does not need a declaration, a single root element, or a DTD. Instead, the tags act as labeled regions that downstream code can locate and extract with a regular expression or a lightweight XML parser.
. Common tag names in the wild include
. The tag names are arbitrary — they are chosen by the prompt author to match the semantic purpose of each section.

Models such as Claude are trained on a large corpus that includes XML-formatted documents, HTML, and various markup languages. As a result, they are very reliable at generating balanced tag pairs when prompted to do so. The Anthropic documentation explicitly recommends using XML tags to separate context, instructions, and output regions in prompts, and this
— placing XML in the input to receive XML in the output — is one reason tag-based output works so consistently with Claude.

Agents and pipelines frequently require a model to produce output that serves several purposes simultaneously. A customer-support bot might need to log its internal reasoning for quality assurance, return a polished answer to the user, and emit a structured action code to trigger a CRM update — all in one API call. Forcing all of this into a JSON object creates friction: long prose strings require careful escaping, embedded newlines must be represented as
, and JSON syntax errors in any field fail the entire parse.
Tag-based output solves this by keeping prose as prose. A

block can span multiple paragraphs with literal newlines, bullet points, or even embedded code fences — none of which require escaping. The tags mark the boundaries. Downstream code extracts each section independently and processes it in the most appropriate way: the reasoning block is logged or hidden, the answer is returned to the user, and the action code is parsed further (possibly as JSON within the tag).
. Extended thinking features in modern APIs expose an internal monologue, but simpler approaches — prompt-driven
sections — let teams inspect how the model reached its conclusion without enabling a separate API capability. This is particularly important for debugging agents that take consequential actions.
Tag content can contain raw newlines, markdown, code, or even nested tags without escaping. JSON requires
for newlines and double-escaped backslashes in code strings.
Prompt symmetry with Claude and similar models.
When XML tags appear in the system prompt to delimit instructions, examples, or context, the model learns to mirror that pattern in output. This makes tag compliance very consistent across requests.

Callers can extract only the tags they need. A pipeline can discard the thinking block, forward the answer block to the user, and route the action block to a tool executor — all without touching the rest of the response.
prefill parameter. Seeding the response with
guarantees that the model opens with that tag before generating any content, eliminating the rare cases where a model might skip the section entirely.
sub-tags, enabling structured multi-step reasoning without a complex JSON schema.
suffices for most use cases. No JSON library, no schema validator, and no deserialization step.
Unlike JSON, the raw tagged text is immediately readable by humans. Logging, debugging, and manual inspection are all easier.

Unlike JSON Schema or the OpenAI structured-output API, there is nothing preventing the model from emitting extra tags, omitting required tags, or misspelling a tag name. Validation is the application's responsibility.
literally inside tag content (e.g. in a code snippet), a strict XML parser will reject the document. Regex workarounds exist but are fragile.
Higher token count than JSON for numeric or short fields.
for short atomic values. The overhead per tag pair is roughly 4–8 tokens depending on tag name length.
No standard tooling for schema definition.
Ecosystems like Pydantic, JSON Schema, or Zod have no XML-tag equivalent. Defining and validating a contract for tagged output requires custom code.

External APIs, databases, and most programmatic consumers expect JSON, not tagged text. Tag-based output is typically an intermediate representation — it must be re-serialized before leaving the system.
While rare with well-prompted models, the model can occasionally forget a closing tag or invent a tag name not in the prompt. Robust parsers must handle this gracefully.

The system prompt instructs the model to structure its output using specific tags. The clearest way is to give an explicit example of the expected format alongside a description of each tag's purpose. A prompt such as:

You are a research assistant. For every question:
1. Wrap your internal reasoning in <thinking> ... </thinking>.
2. Write your final answer inside <answer> ... </answer>.
3. Rate your confidence from 0.0 to 1.0 in <confidence> ... </confidence>.

Always emit all three tags, even for simple questions.

is more reliable than simply saying 'use XML tags' because it names each tag, states its purpose, and sets an expectation that all three must appear. Providing a filled-in example (few-shot) further improves compliance.

Language models generate text token by token. When the model has been trained on large volumes of XML and HTML — as virtually all frontier models have — it assigns high probability to balanced tag pairs once it has generated an opening tag. The key mechanism is
: each generated token narrows the probability distribution of the next. After emitting
, the model is heavily conditioned toward closing that tag at some point before moving on. This is why tag-based output works even without grammar-constrained decoding.
On the receiving end, the application extracts sections by searching for tag boundaries. Two approaches are common:
to match newlines, enabling multi-line tag bodies. This approach is fast and tolerates minor XML invalidity (e.g. unescaped
), but it fails on nested tags of the same name.
Wrap the entire output in a synthetic root element —
. This handles nesting correctly and validates well-formedness, but it will raise an exception if the model output contains unescaped special characters.
Most production systems combine both: attempt XML parsing first, fall back to regex if parsing fails, and log the fallback rate as a quality metric to track prompt drift over time.

The diagram below shows the full pipeline from system prompt through LLM generation to downstream consumption. Notice that the parser sits between the raw model output and any application logic — the application never receives the raw text, only the extracted, typed fields.

attrs.alt: XML tag-based output pipeline: system prompt → LLM → raw output with tags → extractor → typed fields → application

content.text: The most important design point is the

: XML tags that appear in the system prompt to delimit instructions and context signal to the model that the expected output dialect uses the same tag style. This is why Anthropic's prompt engineering guide recommends wrapping context blocks in tags like
— it primes the model to produce tags in the response without an explicit instruction for every field.
When different sections require unescaped text — narratives, code blocks, markdown — tag wrapping is simpler than JSON with escaped strings.
section allows callers to log or audit the model's reasoning without a separate extended-thinking API call.
Systems that already use XML tags in the system prompt (to delimit tool results, documents, or examples) naturally extend that convention to the output.
When the downstream consumer is an existing system that reads XML — an enterprise integration bus, an XSLT transform, or a Java SAX handler — producing tag-based output avoids a conversion step.
Tags have unambiguous open/close markers, making it easy to detect section boundaries in a streaming response and process each section as it arrives, before the full response completes.
For output that must conform to a Pydantic model, JSON Schema, or an OpenAPI response spec, use the provider's structured-output mode or function calling instead.
When the response is a dozen numeric or boolean fields, JSON is more compact, has better ecosystem support, and is trivially typed.
If user-supplied content could appear inside tag bodies (e.g. a model that quotes user text in its output), tag injection and XML entity attacks become a concern that JSON avoids entirely.
Implementing tag-based output involves three steps: designing the tag schema, writing the system prompt, and writing the extraction layer. Each step has concrete decisions that affect reliability.
Decide what distinct regions the response should contain. Avoid tags that overlap semantically.
. XML tag names are case-sensitive; consistency matters for the extraction regex.
Decide whether tags are optional or required.
Document this in the system prompt. Required tags simplify the extraction code; optional tags add flexibility but require fallback logic.
Avoid nesting the same tag name.
) breaks simple regex extraction. Use numbered names (
) or an outer container tag instead.

Be explicit about tag names, the purpose of each, and whether they are required. Include a fully worked example. For Claude, use the Anthropic-recommended style of also wrapping context in XML tags to reinforce the pattern:

You are a code reviewer. When reviewing a diff:

<instructions>
  Always respond with exactly three sections:
  <issues>   — A bullet list of problems found (empty list if none).
  <verdict>  — One of: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | COMMENT
  <rationale>— One paragraph explaining the verdict.
</instructions>

Example output format:
<issues>
- Missing null check on line 42
- Variable `x` shadows outer scope
</issues>
<verdict>REQUEST_CHANGES</verdict>
<rationale>
The null check omission on line 42 could cause a NullPointerException
under normal production traffic.
</rationale>
Build a dedicated parser function rather than inline regex calls. This centralizes fallback handling and makes it easy to add validation:

import re
from typing import Optional

def extract_tag(text: str, tag: str) -> Optional[str]:
    """Extract content from <tag>…</tag>. Returns None if tag absent."""
    pattern = rf"<{tag}>(.*?)</{tag}>"
    match = re.search(pattern, text, re.DOTALL)
    return match.group(1).strip() if match else None

def parse_review(raw: str) -> dict:
    issues_raw = extract_tag(raw, "issues") or ""
    issues = [line.lstrip("- ").strip()
              for line in issues_raw.splitlines()
              if line.strip() and line.strip() != "-"]
    verdict = extract_tag(raw, "verdict")
    rationale = extract_tag(raw, "rationale")
    if verdict not in {"APPROVE", "REQUEST_CHANGES", "COMMENT"}:
        raise ValueError(f"Unexpected verdict: {verdict!r}")
    return {"issues": issues, "verdict": verdict, "rationale": rationale}
The simplest use case: extract a single tag from the model output.

import re
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM = """
You are a concise summarizer. Wrap your summary in <summary> tags.
Do not include anything outside the tags.
"""

def summarize(text: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text
    match = re.search(r"<summary>(.*?)</summary>", raw, re.DOTALL)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError("Model did not return a <summary> tag")
    return match.group(1).strip()

# Usage
article = (
    "Recent studies have found that daily exercise of as little as 20 minutes "
    "can significantly reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, "
    "and certain cancers. Researchers at Harvard Medical School analyzed data from "
    "over 500,000 participants across 12 countries over a 15-year period."
)
print(summarize(article))
# → 'A large multi-country study found that 20 minutes of daily exercise
#    substantially lowers the risk of heart disease, diabetes, and some cancers.'
prefill to guarantee that the model starts its response with the opening tag, preventing any preamble text:

import re
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM = """
You are a reasoning assistant. Structure your response exactly as:
<thinking>
  [your step-by-step reasoning, may be multiple paragraphs]
</thinking>
<answer>
  [your final answer, concise]
</answer>
<confidence>[a float between 0.0 and 1.0]</confidence>
"""

@dataclass
class ReasonedAnswer:
    thinking: str
    answer: str
    confidence: float

def ask(question: str) -> ReasonedAnswer:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": question},
            # Prefill forces the model to start with <thinking>
            {"role": "assistant", "content": "<thinking>"},
        ],
    )
    # The prefill is NOT included in content[0].text;
    # prepend it to reconstruct the full response.
    raw = "<thinking>" + response.content[0].text

    def extract(tag: str) -> str:
        m = re.search(rf"<{tag}>(.*?)</{tag}>", raw, re.DOTALL)
        return m.group(1).strip() if m else ""

    thinking = extract("thinking")
    answer = extract("answer")
    conf_str = extract("confidence")
    try:
        confidence = float(conf_str)
    except ValueError:
        confidence = 0.0

    return ReasonedAnswer(thinking=thinking, answer=answer, confidence=confidence)

result = ask("What is the capital of France?")
print(f"Answer: {result.answer}")
print(f"Confidence: {result.confidence}")
# Answer: Paris
# Confidence: 0.99

A document intelligence pipeline at a legal-tech company processes incoming contracts and extracts key metadata to populate a deal-management database. Each contract is up to 50 pages, and the pipeline calls a frontier model to extract parties, effective date, governing law, and risk flags. JSON output was tried first but caused frequent parse failures because the contracts contained embedded quotation marks, percent signs, and currency symbols that the model inconsistently escaped.
The team switched to tag-based output. The system prompt defines seven tags —

— and instructs the model to quote verbatim text from the document inside the relevant tag. Verbatim quotes frequently contain special characters, but the regex extractor is forgiving whereas the JSON parser was not.

import re
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

CONTRACT_SYSTEM = """
You extract structured metadata from legal contracts.
Output exactly the following tags in order:

<parties>Comma-separated list of all party names</parties>
<effective-date>The contract effective date (ISO 8601 if determinable)</effective-date>
<governing-law>Jurisdiction clause, verbatim</governing-law>
<payment-terms>Payment schedule summary</payment-terms>
<termination>Termination conditions summary</termination>
<risk-flags>
  Bullet list of unusual clauses or red flags. Empty list if none.
</risk-flags>
<confidence>0.0–1.0 overall extraction confidence</confidence>

Quote exact contract language where instructed.
Do not add commentary outside the tags.
"""

TAG_NAMES = [
    "parties", "effective-date", "governing-law",
    "payment-terms", "termination", "risk-flags", "confidence",
]

def extract_contract(contract_text: str) -> dict:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=CONTRACT_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": contract_text}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text
    result = {}
    for tag in TAG_NAMES:
        pattern = rf"<{tag}>(.*?)</{tag}>"
        match = re.search(pattern, raw, re.DOTALL)
        result[tag] = match.group(1).strip() if match else None

    # Coerce confidence to float
    try:
        result["confidence"] = float(result["confidence"] or 0)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        result["confidence"] = 0.0

    missing = [t for t in TAG_NAMES if result.get(t) is None]
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Model omitted required tags: {missing}")

    return result

This pipeline processes about 3,000 contracts per day. The tag parse failure rate is under 0.3 %, compared to over 8 % for the previous JSON-based approach on the same contract corpus. On the rare failures, the application queues the document for human review rather than silently dropping data.
metacharacter does not match newline characters. This silently produces
for any tag whose body spans multiple lines — which is nearly every substantive block.

# Wrong — fails silently for multi-line tag bodies
match = re.search(r"<answer>(.*?)</answer>", text)

# Correct
match = re.search(r"<answer>(.*?)</answer>", text, re.DOTALL)
Not handling missing or misspelled tags
Models occasionally omit a tag or misspell it (
that is hard to trace back to the model response.

# Wrong — crashes if tag is absent
raw_answer = re.search(r"<answer>(.*?)</answer>", text, re.DOTALL).group(1)

# Correct — return None and handle upstream
match = re.search(r"<answer>(.*?)</answer>", text, re.DOTALL)
raw_answer = match.group(1).strip() if match else None
Using strict XML parsing on unescaped model output
If the model quotes source code or prose containing
inside a tag body, wrapping in a root element and passing to
. Always catch this and fall back to regex:
Placing user-controlled text directly in tag names
Never interpolate user input into the tag name itself:
. A malicious user could supply a tag name like
, injecting extra fields into the structured output. Tag
should always be constants defined by the application.
Include a fully worked example in the system prompt.
Show the complete expected output format with representative content in each tag. This is more reliable than a description alone.
Consistent casing eliminates a category of mismatch bugs. Never mix
State required vs optional tags explicitly.
Tell the model which tags must always appear. For required tags, assert their presence in the extraction layer and surface a clear error if missing.
Validate tag content, not just tag presence.
tag should be checked against an allowlist of valid values. A
tag should be verified to parse as a float in [0.0, 1.0].
If the first tag must always appear, seed the assistant's response with the opening tag using the
prefill parameter. This eliminates preamble and guarantees the model begins with the expected structure.
Track how often your extractor falls back from XML parsing to regex. A rising rate is an early signal of model drift, prompt regression, or a distribution shift in input content.
Keep tag content pure text when possible.

Avoid designing schemas where the model must emit JSON or YAML inside a tag body. Mixed formats compound parsing complexity and failure modes. If a tag must contain structured data, use CDATA or a dedicated structured-output call for that section.
Each tag pair adds tokens proportional to the tag name length. A tag name of five characters (e.g.

, since the angle brackets and slash are separate tokens. A response with six tag pairs adds approximately 40–60 tokens of structural overhead — about 1–3 % of a 2,000-token response. This is negligible at normal scales but matters when processing millions of short requests per day.

JSON output has a similar fixed overhead (braces, colons, quotes) but imposes additional overhead on long prose values due to JSON string escaping. For responses where tag bodies contain multi-paragraph prose, tag-based output is typically
token-efficient than JSON because it avoids the per-character escape overhead.
Regex extraction is extremely fast — on the order of microseconds per response for typical response lengths. XML parsing via

is slightly slower due to DOM construction but still sub-millisecond for responses under 10,000 characters. Neither approach is a throughput bottleneck in any realistic production system; the model API latency dominates by three to four orders of magnitude.

When processing large document corpora in batch (e.g. extracting metadata from 100,000 contracts), the throughput bottleneck is the API rate limit and per-token cost, not the parsing step. Tag-based extraction is stateless and trivially parallelizable: each response can be parsed independently in a separate thread or process without shared state.
For very high-volume pipelines, consider caching the compiled regex patterns at module level (not inside the extraction function) to avoid recompilation on every call:
module in the Python standard library handles well-formed XML without any dependencies. Wrap the model output in a root element, then use
lxml for large or complex responses
library is a drop-in replacement for
with better error messages, XPath 1.0 support, and significantly faster parsing for large documents. Install with
. It is especially useful when the model output contains nested tags or when you need to traverse the element tree rather than just accessing named top-level elements.
The Anthropic Python SDK supports seeding the assistant's response via the
entry as the last message causes the model to continue generating from that point, guaranteeing the response starts with the opening tag. The SDK
include the prefill in the returned
, so the caller must prepend it manually before parsing.

The same tag-based prompting strategy works with the OpenAI API and other providers — it is purely a prompting technique, not an API feature. Prefill is not available in the OpenAI chat-completions API, so applications relying on guaranteed tag opening must use explicit instructions and few-shot examples instead.
Each structured output approach occupies a different position on the axes of enforcement strength, prose-friendliness, and ecosystem support:
The key insight from this table is that
XML tags and JSON are complementary rather than competing

. Tag-based output excels when individual sections contain long prose. JSON excels when the entire response is a tree of short, typed values. A common hybrid pattern uses tag-based output to separate prose sections (thinking, rationale, citations) while encoding the structured payload as a JSON blob within a dedicated
tag — getting the best of both approaches.

If user-supplied text is interpolated into the model's prompt and the model quotes it verbatim in a tag body, a malicious user can inject closing tags followed by content that impersonates a system-level field. For example, if a user submits the string
, and the model echoes it inside an
Sanitize inputs before passing them to the model.
from user-supplied text before it enters the prompt.
Extract the model's own tags, not re-quoted user content.
Design prompts so the model synthesizes new content rather than echoing user input verbatim.
, check the extracted value against a known set of valid options. Reject unexpected values rather than forwarding them downstream.
If the model output is parsed with a full XML parser, XML entity expansion is a potential vector when user input can influence the content. By default,
does not resolve external entities, but
in its default configuration does. When using

Tag compliance rates can degrade after a model provider updates the underlying model weights. A model that followed tag instructions reliably on version N may fail more often on version N+1 if the updated RLHF training has altered the model's instruction-following behavior. Mitigations:
Pin to a specific model version in production and test new versions before upgrading.
Instrument extraction code to track the rate of missing or malformed tags per model version.
Add a canary test that checks tag compliance on a fixed prompt-response pair as part of the deployment pipeline.
XML-style tag-based output labels named regions in a model response with paired tags, enabling selective, prose-friendly extraction without full JSON syntax.
Prompt symmetry matters: placing XML tags in the system prompt to delimit instructions and context strongly encourages the model to mirror that structure in its output.
in every regex extraction pattern; tag bodies almost always span multiple lines.
Combine XML parsing with a regex fallback: attempt
, and fall back to regex to tolerate unescaped special characters in tag bodies.
The Anthropic SDK's assistant prefill guarantees the response starts with the specified opening tag, eliminating preamble and improving extraction reliability for critical fields.
Tag-based and JSON output are complementary: use tags for long prose sections and JSON for structured numeric or boolean payloads, potentially embedding JSON inside a dedicated tag.
Sanitize user-supplied content before it enters the prompt, validate extracted field values against allowlists, and disable XML entity expansion in lxml to prevent injection and XXE attacks.
Monitor tag compliance rates across model versions and add canary tests to catch regressions when the provider updates model weights.
